environmental_storytelling.example: Progression through Fun World stages (main park → haunted house → pirate ship → pits) implies story escalation from institutional control through criminal activity to final confrontation
Mix of security guards, mascots, and pirates in same environment communicates corruption; no pure 'safe' area remains
Haunted house and pirate ship stages carry specific visual themes that establish danger zones distinct from entertainment spaces
Pirate ship exists as separate stage suggesting distinct criminal territory within/adjacent to theme park

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Actively attack player; represent institutional opposition
Actively attack player; formerly benign entities turned antagonistic
Actively attack player; criminal faction operating within/around park
Can move freely, attack enemies; possess unique special attacks

notes: Bebe's Kids communicates world-state primarily through location progression and enemy faction variety. The core narrative visual is the corruption of a family-entertainment space (Fun World/Disneyland analog) into a combat zone. Three distinct antagonist factions—institutional security, corrupted entertainment entities, and criminal elements—establish a world where legitimate authority, entertainment, and criminality have all turned hostile to the player. Visual communication is minimalist due to SNES technical constraints, relying on stage themes and enemy archetypes rather than detailed environmental detail. The game does not appear to use color identity or elaborate sigils for faction distinction; faction identification relies on enemy type recognition (uniforms, costumes, pirate attire) and stage geography.
